text.title: Unit Testing Antipatterns
text.summary: Failure modes that produce brittle, misleading, or unreliable tests.

antipattern.description: Testing implementation details: asserting that a private method was called or that internal state changed.
antipattern.reason: Tests break when the implementation is refactored even though the behavior is unchanged. Tests should assert observable outputs and side effects, not internal mechanics.

antipattern.description: Over-mocking: replacing every collaborator with a MagicMock, then asserting on mock calls.
antipattern.reason: The test verifies the mock's configuration, not the behavior. MagicMock with no spec accepts calls to nonexistent methods, so tests pass even when the interface drifts.

antipattern.description: Shared mutable state: class-level lists or dicts that tests append to without reset.
antipattern.reason: Execution-order dependency makes tests fail intermittently and only in certain run permutations. pytest-randomly surfaces these on day one.

antipattern.description: Giant test methods: one test function with 10 asserts checking different behaviors.
antipattern.reason: When the first assertion fails, the test runner skips the rest. The failure message does not indicate which behavior broke. Split into one test per behavior.

antipattern.description: No assertions: a test that runs code and passes regardless of output.
antipattern.reason: The test provides false coverage confidence. Static analysis cannot detect this; require at least one explicit assert per test in code review.
Exception catches everything including KeyboardInterrupt and SystemExit. Always assert the specific exception class expected.

antipattern.description: time.sleep() in tests for timing-dependent behavior.
antipattern.reason: Sleep-based tests are slow and still flaky if the machine is under load. Use freezegun.freeze_time, asyncio.sleep(0), or event-based waiting instead.

antipattern.description: @patch decorator order reversed from parameter order.
antipattern.reason: @patch decorators apply bottom-up to parameters. @patch("a") @patch("b") def test(self, mock_b, mock_a). Reversing the mental model produces off-by-one mock assignments that are hard to debug.
